input.headline: The receipt myth and why you shouldn't buy a warranty | Money newsletter

raw.headline: The receipt myth and why you shouldn't buy a warranty | Money newsletter

winner: Why should you always refuse a warranty at the checkout counter? Because the receipt says it covers everything... except for your peace of mind!

candidates.text: Why should you always refuse a warranty at the checkout counter? Because the receipt says it covers everything... except for your peace of mind!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.0, "reason": "The joke is concise, surprising, and ties directly to the headline with a clever twist on 'covers everything'."}

candidates.text: Why did the receipt go to therapy? Because it was tired of being unclaimed and constantly worrying about warranty claims!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 7.5, "reason": "The joke is clever with a play on 'warranty claims' and 'unclaimed,' but the setup could be tighter for maximum surprise."}

candidates.text: Why did the cashier refuse to sell a warranty for an imaginary purchase? Because he didn't want to be held receipt-sponsible!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat clever with the wordplay on 'receipt-sponsible,' but it's a bit forced and lacks specificity to the topic of warranties and receipts. It's not offensive, but it falls short in surprise and natural flow."}

candidates.text: Why should you never buy a warranty for your luck? Because even receipts are starting to doubt its effectiveness!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat clever with the twist about receipts doubting effectiveness, but it's a bit forced and lacks clarity. The connection between warranties, luck, and receipts isn't fully explained, making it less surprising and specific."}

candidates.text: When asked if a receipt could guarantee happiness, the cashier said, "Well, I can't promise it, but I can sell you a warranty for one.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat relevant to the topic but lacks strong surprise or specificity. It plays on the idea of warranties and receipts, but the punchline feels a bit forced and not fully aligned with the 'receipt myth' theme."}
